sections.Ventilator Induced Lung Injury (VILI): VILI = acute lung injury induced/aggravated by MV, histopathologically indistinguishable from ARDS. Two main mechanisms: lung heterogeneity - collapsed/oedematous alveoli act as 'stress raisers' for neighbouring open units, which overdistend during tidal changes; atelectrauma - repetitive collapse/reopening causing high dynamic strain/shear stress. Biotrauma = inflammatory mediator release (TNF-a, IL-6, IL-8) from stress/strain. High distending pressure raises transmural vascular pressure/decreases interstitial pressure (increased infiltration); surfactant depletion increases surface tension; overinflation damages the alveolo-capillary barrier. Estimation: Pplat and driving pressure (DP=Pplat-PEEP=VT/Crs); transpulmonary pressure PL=alveolar-pleural (Ppl~oesophageal Pes): PLendinsp=Pplat-Pes(end-insp); PLendexp=PEEP-Pes(end-exp). Strain: static=VT; dynamic=VT/FRC (better reflects VILI risk, no widespread FRC measurement). Mechanical power incorporates VT, DP, flow, resistance, RR, PEEP; RR is a component. Targets: VT 4-6ml/kg PBW; Pplat<30; VT/FRC 1.5-2.0; PLendinsp<20-25; PLendexp 0-2 (negative=collapse); DP<14 (strongest mortality predictor); DPL probably <12; prone >16h early in severe ARDS; NMB. P-SILI: vigorous effort lowers pleural pressure, raises PL/VT; pendelluft = more-negative pleural pressure in dependent regions causes early gas shift from non-dependent to dependent, worsening injury; negative intrathoracic pressure raises venous return and pulmonary vascular transmural pressure -> oedema; asynchrony (double triggering) raises PL/VT. Monitoring: gold standard oesophageal catheter; non-invasive P0.1, Pocc. PEEP improves homogeneity but response NOT uniform - recruitable lungs benefit, non-recruitable worsen strain; assess recruitability first. Recruitment manoeuvres: sustained inflation (CPAP 30-40cmH2O for 30-40s); evidence mixed, not routine. HFOV: very low VT, freq 3-15Hz; two large RCTs no benefit/possible harm; meta-analysis benefit in severe ARDS (P/F<100) but worse at higher P/F. Prone: PROSEVA (P/F<150, >=16h/day) improved survival; prone + low VT most effective for mortality; early within 36h. Discontinue prone: P/F>=150, FiO2<=0.6, PEEP<=10 for >=4h. NMB: ACURASYS (340, moderate-severe) decreased 90-day mortality; ROSE (1006, severe, 48h cisatracurium) no survival benefit; selective use in early severe ARDS. ECCO2R: enables ultra-protective VT (3-4ml/kg) when hypercapnia limits ventilation. ECMO: EOLIA 11% mortality reduction (not significant). Most effective strategies: low tidal volume and prone positioning.
sections.Ventilator Induced Diaphragmatic Dysfunc: VIDD = loss of diaphragmatic force related to MV, not fully explained by sepsis/drugs. Unlike ICU-acquired weakness, phrenic nerve transmission and neuromuscular junction are normal/increased - the problem is within muscle fibres. Prevalence: ~60% severe weakness at first SBT; ~2x ICU-acquired weakness; up to 67% WITHOUT apparent ICU-AW still have VIDD; contributes to weaning failure (>50%). 4 injury mechanisms: over-assistance (abolished contraction -> rapid disuse atrophy via proteolysis, cardinal mechanism); under-assistance (excessive load -> injury); asynchrony with eccentric contractions; longitudinal atrophy from high PEEP (diaphragm shortens, sarcomeres decrease; on PEEP reduction, overstretched). Timeline: 12h controlled MV -> ~15% fibre reduction; 18-24h -> ~30%. Diagnosis: exclude other causes first; assess strength - MIP (easy, no equipment, but all inspiratory muscles, effort-dependent); diaphragm-specific - twitch Pdi (18cmH2O, GOLD STANDARD, magnetic phrenic stimulation, no cooperation, valid passive), Ptr,stim (7cmH2O, alternative when oesophageal pressure unavailable), TFdi (30%, ultrasound, specific, short learning curve), EAdi (normal values unknown). TFdi = (thickness end-insp - end-exp)/end-exp; excursion<1cm and TFdi<30% associated with failure. Prevention: shorten MV, minimise sedation, avoid NMB, adjust PEEP, early assisted modes, address dyssynchrony, avoid both excessive AND inadequate assist; PAV+/NAVA may protect. Phrenic stimulation trial: did not increase weaning success but increased MIP.
sections.Ventilator Associated Pneumonia (VAP): Definition: pneumonia >=48h after intubation/invasive MV. ~half of hospital-acquired pneumonia; 5-40% of ventilated patients. Daily risk highest days 5-9; cumulative incidence rises with duration. Mortality: 36.6% per INICC, up to 50%; varies by country/ICU/criteria/organism. Diagnosis: positive lower-respiratory cultures + deterioration + infection signs + new infiltrates; classic indicators (fever, purulent secretions, leukocytosis, CXR, cultures, CPIS) have poor specificity. Prevention: most effective = avoid intubation/minimise MV duration; NIV/high-flow help; lighten sedation, SBTs, timely extubation, mobilisation; head-of-bed elevation, hand hygiene; stress ulcer prophylaxis, subglottic drainage, selective digestive decontamination, chlorhexidine remain CONTROVERSIAL. MDR risk factors: local resistance >25%, parenteral antibiotics in 90 days, hospital stay >5 days, prior colonisation, ARDS/septic shock/RRT before VAP; MRSA: local rate >20% or colonisation; MDR Pseudomonas: local resistance >10% or colonisation. Treatment: majority Gram-negative (Pseudomonas, E. coli, Klebsiella, Acinetobacter); S. aureus most common Gram-positive; rising ESBL/carbapenemase; 50-67% Acinetobacter carbapenem-resistant; fungi rare except Aspergillus (late-onset, post-influenza/COVID); rapid diagnostics can reduce antibiotic use/improve outcomes.

questions.question: In a lung with heterogeneous aeration containing both collapsed and normally aerated alveoli, what happens to the aerated alveoli next to the collapsed units during tidal ventilation?
options: A: They contribute nothing, as heterogeneity does not affect regional stress
B: They act as 'stress raisers', overdistending during tidal volume changes
C: They are protected, as neighbouring collapsed units absorb the applied pressure
D: They stay entirely unaffected by the state of neighbouring alveoli
E: They collapse further in response to the neighbouring overdistension
questions.explanation: Collapsed or oedema-filled alveoli act as 'stress raisers' for neighbouring open units, which overdistend during tidal changes - concentrating injurious stress at these interfaces. The other options deny or reverse this regional stress concentration.
questions.topic: VILI heterogeneity stress raisers collapsed alveoli overdistension

questions.question: Alveolar units that repetitively collapse at end-expiration and reopen with each breath are subject to which injurious mechanism?
options: A: A pattern with no described injurious mechanism
B: Atelectrauma, from high dynamic strain and shear stress as units recruit and derecruit
C: Volutrauma, caused solely by excessive tidal volume regardless of recruitment
D: Oxygen toxicity, unrelated to mechanical forces
E: Barotrauma, caused solely by excessive absolute airway pressure
questions.explanation: Repetitive collapse and reopening generates high dynamic strain and shear stress - atelectrauma - distinct from volutrauma or barotrauma, though the mechanisms interrelate. The other options misattribute the injury or deny it.
questions.topic: atelectrauma repetitive collapse reopening dynamic strain shear stress

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology of VILI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: High distending pressures can increase infiltration by raising transmural vascular pressure or lowering interstitial pressure.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Lung overinflation can damage the alveolo-capillary barrier, raising epithelial and endothelial permeability.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Ventilation-induced surfactant depletion decreases alveolar surface tension, protecting against collapse.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Leukocyte activation and release of mediators such as TNF-alpha, IL-6 and IL-8 contribute to biotrauma.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is reversed - surfactant depletion/inactivation INCREASES surface tension, promoting collapse rather than protecting against it.
questions.topic: biotrauma mediators surfactant surface tension infiltration permeability

questions.question: A patient with ARDS has an end-inspiratory plateau pressure of 28 cmH2O and an end-inspiratory oesophageal pressure of 18 cmH2O. What is the estimated end-inspiratory transpulmonary pressure, and how does it compare with typical protective targets?
options: A: It cannot be calculated without separately measuring the PEEP
B: It is found by adding, not subtracting, the oesophageal pressure
C: 46 cmH2O, far above target, reflecting the airway pressure alone
D: 18 cmH2O, equal to the oesophageal pressure alone
E: 10 cmH2O (28 minus 18), within the typical target below 20-25 cmH2O
questions.explanation: PLendinsp = Pplat minus Pes(end-insp) = 28 - 18 = 10 cmH2O, within the typical target below 20-25 cmH2O - showing that plateau pressure alone can overestimate lung-distending pressure when chest wall elastance contributes. The other options miscalculate or misdefine it.
questions.topic: transpulmonary pressure PLendinsp calculation oesophageal pressure plateau target

questions.question: Two patients are both ventilated at 500 mL tidal volume. Patient A has an FRC of 2500 mL; Patient B, with severe ARDS, has an FRC of 1000 mL. How do their dynamic strains (VT/FRC) compare?
options: A: Patient A has the higher dynamic strain, owing to the larger absolute FRC
B: Patient B has higher dynamic strain (0.5 vs 0.2), reflecting the smaller aerated lung
C: Both are identical, since static strain equals VT and dynamic strain ignores FRC
D: Static and dynamic strain are always numerically identical whatever the FRC
E: Dynamic strain is VT multiplied by FRC, not divided by it
questions.explanation: Dynamic strain = VT/FRC, so Patient B's smaller aerated lung gives 0.5 versus Patient A's 0.2 despite identical tidal volumes - illustrating why dynamic strain reflects VILI risk better than tidal volume alone. The other options misstate the relationship.
questions.topic: static dynamic strain VT FRC ratio VILI risk aerated lung

questions.question: Regarding targets used to limit VILI risk:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The mechanical power target for minimising VILI has not been clearly established.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A negative end-expiratory transpulmonary pressure suggests alveolar overdistension at end-expiration.

statements.label: C
statements.text: An end-inspiratory transpulmonary pressure below 20-25 cmH2O and a lung driving pressure below about 12 cmH2O are considered protective.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A dynamic strain (VT/FRC) above 1.5-2.0 is associated with VILI, but routine FRC measurement is not widely available.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is reversed - a negative end-expiratory transpulmonary pressure reflects alveolar COLLAPSE, not overdistension.
questions.topic: VILI targets PLendexp collapse mechanical power unknown

questions.question: In a patient with acute lung injury making vigorous inspiratory efforts, gas moves from non-dependent to dependent lung regions early in inspiration, distending the dependent regions. What is this, and what causes it?
options: A: Barotrauma, from excessive positive pressure ventilation
B: Auto-PEEP, from inadequate expiratory time
C: Diffusion limitation, from a thickened alveolar-capillary membrane
D: Pendelluft, from more-negative pleural pressure in dependent lung regions
E: Double triggering, from strong effort triggering the ventilator twice
questions.explanation: Pendelluft occurs when vigorous effort produces a more-negative pleural pressure in dependent than non-dependent regions, shifting gas from non-dependent to dependent regions early in inspiration - a mechanism of regional injury in P-SILI. The other options describe unrelated phenomena.
questions.topic: pendelluft P-SILI pleural pressure dependent non-dependent regions

questions.question: Besides directly raising transpulmonary pressure and tidal volume, how else can vigorous spontaneous breathing promote pulmonary oedema in an injured lung?
options: A: By reducing cardiac output to zero, removing all pulmonary vascular pressure change
B: By directly causing bronchospasm, unrelated to any vascular mechanism
C: By a mechanism that has never been proposed in the literature
D: Negative intrathoracic pressure raises venous return and vascular transmural pressure
E: By lowering venous return and reducing pulmonary vascular pressures
questions.explanation: Negative intrathoracic pressure from vigorous effort raises venous return and the transmural pressure of the pulmonary vessels, favouring oedema - an additional P-SILI mechanism alongside pendelluft. The other options reverse or deny the mechanism.
questions.topic: P-SILI venous return transmural pressure pulmonary vessels oedema

questions.question: Beyond vigorous effort itself, which patient-ventilator interaction can further raise transpulmonary pressures and tidal volumes, undermining a lung-protective strategy?
options: A: The application of low levels of PEEP alone
B: A completely passive, sedated patient with no effort
C: Double triggering and breath stacking
D: A reduced respiratory rate alone, with no triggering change
E: Perfect synchrony between effort and delivery
questions.explanation: Double triggering and breath stacking can raise transpulmonary pressures and tidal volumes beyond lung-protective targets, negating an otherwise protective strategy. The other options describe conditions that would not augment distending pressures.
questions.topic: P-SILI double triggering breath stacking asynchrony lung protective

questions.question: In a patient under assisted ventilation, which non-invasive techniques estimate potentially injurious inspiratory efforts without an oesophageal catheter?
options: A: Airway occlusion pressure (P0.1) and single-breath expiratory occlusion pressure (Pocc)
B: There are no non-invasive alternatives to oesophageal manometry for this purpose
C: Arterial blood gas analysis alone, with no pressure measurement of any kind
D: Only invasive pulmonary artery catheterisation performed at the bedside
E: Chest radiography alone, with no functional measurement of effort
questions.explanation: While the gold standard uses an oesophageal catheter, non-invasive P0.1 and single-breath expiratory occlusion pressure (Pocc) can estimate potentially injurious efforts. The other options deny or misstate the available techniques.
questions.topic: inspiratory effort monitoring P0.1 Pocc non-invasive oesophageal catheter

questions.question: Regarding lung recruitability and the response to increased PEEP in ARDS:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In a non-recruitable lung, raising PEEP worsens strain by overdistending already-aerated alveoli without meaningful recruitment.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Assessing recruitability before selecting PEEP is worthwhile, since the response is not uniform.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Patients with ARDS respond uniformly to a PEEP increase, all showing similar recruitment.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In a recruitable lung, raising PEEP reduces mechanical strain and atelectrauma by recruiting collapsed units.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is false - patients do not respond uniformly to a PEEP increase, which is exactly why recruitability should be assessed first.
questions.topic: PEEP recruitability responder non-responder atelectrauma overdistension

questions.question: How is a 'sustained inflation' recruitment manoeuvre performed?
options: A: By switching to volume control and delivering a single 20 mL/kg breath
B: By switching to CPAP and applying 30-40 cmH2O for 30-40 seconds
C: It is a purely theoretical technique never described in practice
D: By rapidly cycling PEEP between 0 and 5 cmH2O for one hour
E: By applying negative airway pressure for several minutes
questions.explanation: Sustained inflation involves switching to CPAP and increasing pressure to 30-40 cmH2O for 30-40 seconds to reopen collapsed units. The other options misdescribe the manoeuvre or deny its existence.
questions.topic: recruitment manoeuvre sustained inflation CPAP pressure duration

questions.question: What does current evidence indicate about high-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV) in ARDS?
options: A: No overall benefit in two trials; possible benefit only in severe ARDS (P/F below 100)
B: Two large trials showed clear benefit in all ARDS, so it should be routine
C: HFOV removes the need for any conventional ventilator settings
D: HFOV improves mortality only in mild ARDS (P/F above 300)
E: HFOV has never been studied in randomised trials
questions.explanation: Two large trials found no benefit and possible harm overall, but a meta-analysis found the mortality association varied with severity - benefit in severe ARDS (P/F below 100) but worse mortality at higher P/F. The other options overstate or deny the evidence.
questions.topic: HFOV evidence severe ARDS P/F subgroup mortality

questions.question: In PROSEVA, proning severe ARDS (P/F below 150) improved survival. What daily duration was used, and what does later evidence suggest about combining prone with other strategies?
options: A: At least 16 hours/day; prone should be delayed until day 7 of severe ARDS
B: 30 minutes/day; prone should never be combined with other lung-protective strategies
C: 2 hours/week; combining strategies adds no benefit over prone alone
D: 16+ hours/day; prone plus low tidal volume is most effective for moderate-severe ARDS
E: At least 16 hours/day; prone is equally effective combined with high tidal volume
questions.explanation: PROSEVA used at least 16 hours/day; a network meta-analysis found prone plus low tidal volume most effective for reducing mortality in moderate-severe ARDS, with guidelines recommending early institution (within 36h) alongside lung-protective ventilation. The other options misstate the duration or the combination.
questions.topic: prone positioning PROSEVA duration low tidal volume network meta-analysis

questions.question: Which combination of criteria supports discontinuing prone-position sessions in ARDS?
options: A: A P/F below 100, indicating the patient has stabilised
B: There are no defined criteria for discontinuing proning
C: P/F at or above 150, FiO2 at or below 0.6 and PEEP at or below 10, held for at least 4 hours
D: A single P/F at or above 100 measured during a prone session
E: Automatic discontinuation after exactly 24 hours of proning, whatever the oxygenation
questions.explanation: Discontinuation criteria include P/F at or above 150, FiO2 at or below 0.6, and PEEP at or below 10 cmH2O, maintained for at least 4 hours after the last session - sustained improvement rather than a single value or fixed time. The other options misstate the thresholds.
questions.topic: prone discontinuation criteria P/F FiO2 PEEP sustained improvement

questions.question: Two major trials examined early neuromuscular blockade in ARDS with differing results. What did they find, and what is a reasonable interpretation?
options: A: ROSE found a clear mortality benefit while the earlier trial found harm
B: An earlier trial cut 90-day mortality but ROSE showed none; selective use is reasonable given P-SILI risk
C: Both trials found blockade harmful, so it should never be used in ARDS
D: Both trials found clear survival benefit, so blockade should be routine and continuous in all ARDS
E: Neither trial found any effect on any outcome
questions.explanation: An earlier trial (340 patients, moderate-severe ARDS) reduced 90-day mortality, while ROSE (1006, severe ARDS) showed no survival benefit; given the conflicting results and P-SILI risk, selective use in early severe ARDS is prudent rather than routine. The other options misstate the trials.
questions.topic: neuromuscular blockade ACURASYS ROSE trial selective use P-SILI

questions.question: A patient needs ultra-protective very-low tidal volume ventilation but develops hypercapnia and acidosis that impede it. Which extracorporeal strategy specifically addresses this, distinct from treating severe hypoxaemia?
options: A: ECCO2R, used only for severe persistent hypoxaemia rather than hypercapnia or acidosis
B: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation, as the only option here for this problem
C: Extracorporeal CO2 removal (ECCO2R), for ultra-protective tidal volumes in hypercapnia
D: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), as the only extracorporeal option available
E: Neither ECCO2R nor ECMO has a role when hypercapnia limits ventilation in this setting
questions.explanation: ECCO2R specifically addresses hypercapnia/acidosis impeding lung-protective ventilation, enabling ultra-protective very-low tidal volumes (3-4 mL/kg); ECMO by contrast is considered for persistent severe hypoxaemia. The other options misassign or deny the roles.
questions.topic: ECCO2R ECMO hypercapnia hypoxaemia ultra-protective ventilation EOLIA

questions.question: Low tidal volume and prone positioning are the most effective strategies for reducing VILI-associated mortality. Which of the following has NOT shown a similarly clear, consistent mortality benefit?
options: A: Low tidal volume ventilation
B: Prone positioning in severe ARDS
C: None of these; all have equally clear, consistent mortality benefit
D: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation, unproven in two large trials
E: The combination of low tidal volume and prone positioning
questions.explanation: Unlike low tidal volume and prone positioning, HFOV showed no overall benefit and possible harm in two large trials, with benefit only suggested in a severe-ARDS subgroup - not a consistent, broadly applicable benefit. B, C and D do have such benefit.
questions.topic: VILI mortality most effective strategies low tidal volume prone HFOV negative stem

questions.question: All of the following are recognised effects of PEEP on lung homogeneity and mechanics EXCEPT:
options: A: Prevention of end-expiratory collapse of unstable units, reducing atelectrauma
B: A reduction in shear injury at margins between aerated and collapsed tissue
C: A rise in end-inspiratory stress that may or may not bring beneficial recruitment, depending on recruitability
D: An increase in aerated alveoli, improving compliance and homogeneity
E: A guaranteed reduction in mechanical strain in every ARDS patient, whatever the recruitability
questions.explanation: PEEP's effect on strain depends on recruitability - in a non-recruitable lung, raising PEEP increases stress without recruitment, worsening strain rather than guaranteeing a reduction. B, C, D and E are all recognised effects.
questions.topic: PEEP homogeneity negative stem recruitability guaranteed strain EXCEPT

questions.question: How is VIDD distinguished from generalised ICU-acquired weakness in terms of neuromuscular transmission?
options: A: In VIDD, phrenic nerve transmission and junction transduction are impaired, exactly as in ICU-acquired weakness
B: VIDD affects only the neuromuscular junction, not the muscle fibres
C: VIDD and ICU-acquired weakness are simply two names for the same condition
D: VIDD is diagnosed solely by the presence of sepsis
E: In VIDD, phrenic transmission and junction transduction are normal, so the lesion is in the muscle fibres
questions.explanation: In VIDD, phrenic nerve transmission and neuromuscular junction transduction are normal or even increased - the dysfunction arises within the diaphragm muscle fibres (atrophy and contractile dysfunction). The other options conflate or misplace the lesion.
questions.topic: VIDD ICU-acquired weakness phrenic nerve neuromuscular junction muscle fibres

questions.question: Regarding the prevalence and impact of VIDD:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Diaphragmatic weakness contributes significantly to weaning failure, prolonged ICU stay and higher mortality.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Nearly all patients with VIDD also have clinically apparent ICU-acquired weakness, and VIDD essentially never occurs without it.

statements.label: C
statements.text: About 60% of patients have severe diaphragmatic weakness at their first spontaneous breathing trial.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Diaphragmatic dysfunction is roughly twice as frequent as ICU-acquired weakness.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is reversed - up to 67% of patients WITHOUT apparent ICU-acquired weakness still have VIDD, showing substantial dissociation between the two.
questions.topic: VIDD prevalence ICU-acquired weakness dissociation

questions.question: Regarding mechanisms by which ventilation injures the diaphragm:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Over-assistance, abolishing diaphragm contraction, rapidly causes disuse atrophy through increased proteolysis and reduced protein synthesis.

statements.label: B
statements.text: High PEEP can cause longitudinal atrophy by shortening the diaphragm at end-expiration, reducing sarcomeres along the fibre.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Under-assistance, causing excessive load, is a recognised mechanism of injury and contractile dysfunction.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Asynchrony with eccentric contractions (contraction while the diaphragm lengthens) is not a recognised mechanism of injury.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is reversed - eccentric contractions (from ineffective efforts or reverse triggering) ARE a recognised mechanism, the fourth of the four described.
questions.topic: VIDD mechanisms over-assistance under-assistance eccentric longitudinal atrophy

questions.question: Based on experimental data, roughly what reduction in diaphragmatic fibre size occurs after 12 hours, and after 18-24 hours, of fully controlled ventilation?
options: A: Fibre size increases rather than decreases in the first 24 hours
B: A fixed 5% whatever the duration, from 1 hour to several days
C: About 70% after 12 hours, with no further change
D: No measurable reduction before 72 hours
E: About 15% after 12 hours, approaching 30% after 18-24 hours
questions.explanation: 12 hours of controlled ventilation gives about a 15% reduction in fibre size, approaching 30% after 18-24 hours - showing how rapidly disuse atrophy develops once contraction is abolished. The other options misstate the time-course.
questions.topic: diaphragm atrophy timeline 12 hours 15 percent 18-24 hours 30 percent

questions.question: A patient ventilated with high PEEP for days has PEEP acutely reduced during weaning. Based on longitudinal diaphragmatic atrophy, what effect might this have?
options: A: The diaphragm, adapted to a shorter length, may be overstretched beyond its optimum
B: PEEP has no effect on muscle fibre length or sarcomere number
C: Reducing PEEP has no relationship to any muscle's length-tension behaviour
D: Performance improves immediately, since reducing PEEP always benefits the diaphragm
E: Longitudinal atrophy affects only the diaphragm's blood supply, not its geometry
questions.explanation: High PEEP shortens the diaphragm and reduces sarcomeres in series; on acute PEEP reduction, the adapted diaphragm can be overstretched beyond its optimal length, impairing contraction during weaning. The other options deny the length-tension relationship.
questions.topic: longitudinal atrophy PEEP sarcomere length tension weaning

questions.question: Regarding methods to assess diaphragm function:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Maximal inspiratory pressure is easy and needs no special equipment, but assesses all inspiratory muscles rather than the diaphragm specifically and needs patient cooperation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Twitch transdiaphragmatic pressure is diaphragm-specific and needs no cooperation, but requires magnetic stimulation and shows considerable variability.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The diaphragm thickening fraction requires a dedicated nasogastric catheter and a special ventilator.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Endotracheal pressure with phrenic stimulation needs no cooperation and is valid in passive ventilation, but is not diaphragm-specific.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is reversed - the thickening fraction (ultrasound) is easily available with a short learning curve; it is EAdi that needs a dedicated catheter and ventilator.
questions.topic: diaphragm assessment MIP twitch Pdi Ptr stim TFdi EAdi

questions.question: On diaphragm ultrasound, thickness at end-expiration is 1.9 mm and at end-inspiration is 2.5 mm. What is the thickening fraction, and does it suggest adequate effort?
options: A: It cannot be calculated without further measurements
B: About 32% ((2.5 minus 1.9)/1.9); above the 30% threshold, suggesting adequate effort
C: 132% (2.5/1.9), far above any threshold, suggesting excessive effort
D: 0.6 mm (2.5 minus 1.9), in millimetres with no defined threshold
E: About minus 24% ((1.9 minus 2.5)/2.5), a negative value indicating paralysis
questions.explanation: TFdi = (end-inspiratory minus end-expiratory thickness)/end-expiratory = (2.5-1.9)/1.9 ~ 32%; since a value below 30% is associated with weaning failure, this above-threshold result suggests adequate contractile effort. The other options miscalculate the fraction.
questions.topic: TFdi thickening fraction calculation D1 D2 threshold weaning failure

questions.question: What is the gold-standard method for evaluating diaphragmatic strength, and what alternative exists if oesophageal pressure is unavailable?
options: A: Electrical activity of the diaphragm, with well-established normal values for strength
B: Twitch Pdi (<18 cmH2O); if unavailable, endotracheal pressure with phrenic stimulation (<7 cmH2O)
C: Maximal inspiratory pressure alone; the alternative is chest radiography of the chest
D: There is no recognised gold-standard method for assessing diaphragmatic strength
E: Diaphragm ultrasound thickening fraction alone, with no defined threshold for weakness
questions.explanation: The gold standard is twitch Pdi (magnetic phrenic stimulation with oesophageal/gastric catheters), with values below 18 cmH2O suggesting weakness; when oesophageal pressure is unavailable, endotracheal pressure with phrenic stimulation (below 7 cmH2O) is the alternative. The other options misidentify the methods.
questions.topic: twitch Pdi gold standard Ptr stim phrenic stimulation thresholds

questions.question: What is described as the most challenging issue in diaphragm-protective ventilation, and which modes may help address it?
options: A: Choosing the correct antibiotic regimen, best addressed with broad-spectrum prophylaxis
B: Determining the correct FiO2, as oxygen toxicity is the main determinant
C: There is no particularly challenging aspect; all strategies are equally simple
D: Selecting the correct endotracheal tube size, unrelated to diaphragm protection
E: The optimal level of assistance; proportional modes such as PAV+ and NAVA may help
questions.explanation: The optimal level of assistance - avoiding both excessive assistance (disuse atrophy) and inadequate assistance (injurious load) - is the most challenging issue; proportional modes (PAV+, NAVA) may help by improving interaction and ensuring ongoing contraction. The other options are irrelevant to diaphragm protection.
questions.topic: VIDD optimal assist PAV+ NAVA diaphragm protection

questions.question: All of the following are recognised diaphragm-protective ventilator strategies EXCEPT:
options: A: Minimising sedation throughout the period of ventilation
B: Shortening the duration of mechanical ventilation wherever this is feasible
C: Deliberately maximising assistance to ensure complete diaphragm rest
D: Avoiding neuromuscular blocking agents where possible during ventilation
E: Addressing patient-ventilator dyssynchrony when it is detected
questions.explanation: Deliberately maximising assistance for complete rest promotes disuse atrophy - the opposite of protection; the aim is to avoid both excessive and inadequate assistance. B, C, D and E are all recognised protective strategies.
questions.topic: diaphragm protective strategies negative stem maximise assistance disuse atrophy EXCEPT

questions.question: A randomised study of temporary transvenous diaphragm neurostimulation in difficult-to-wean patients found what regarding weaning success and maximal inspiratory pressure?
options: A: It did not increase weaning success, but significantly increased maximal inspiratory pressure
B: It significantly worsened both weaning success and maximal inspiratory pressure
C: It was abandoned before any results due to safety concerns
D: It had no measurable physiological effect of any kind
E: It significantly increased successful weaning with no effect on maximal inspiratory pressure
questions.explanation: The stimulation did not increase successful weaning but was associated with a significant increase in maximal inspiratory pressure - suggesting physiological reversal of diaphragm dysfunction even without a demonstrated clinical weaning effect. The other options misstate the finding.
questions.topic: phrenic stimulation trial weaning success maximal inspiratory pressure

questions.question: How is ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) defined in terms of timing relative to intubation?
options: A: Pneumonia occurring only after extubation from mechanical ventilation
B: Pneumonia occurring at any time before intubation of the patient
C: Any pneumonia during an ICU admission, whatever the ventilation status of the patient
D: Pneumonia at least 48 hours after intubation and invasive ventilation
E: Pneumonia occurring only within the first 24 hours of intubation and ventilation
questions.explanation: VAP is defined as pneumonia occurring at least 48 hours after intubation and invasive ventilation - distinguishing it from pneumonia present at or very soon after intubation. The other options misstate the timing.
questions.topic: VAP definition timing 48 hours intubation

questions.question: During which period of ventilation is the daily risk of VAP highest, and how does cumulative incidence relate to duration?
options: A: Daily risk is highest only after 30 days of ventilation
B: Daily risk is highest in the first 24 hours, and cumulative incidence falls as duration rises
C: Daily risk is higher between days 5-9, while cumulative incidence rises with total ventilation duration
D: Cumulative incidence bears no relationship to ventilation duration
E: Daily risk is constant throughout, with no time-dependent pattern
questions.explanation: The daily risk of VAP is higher between days 5-9, while cumulative incidence continues to rise with total ventilation duration - reinforcing why minimising duration is a key prevention strategy. The other options misstate the pattern.
questions.topic: VAP epidemiology daily risk 5-9 days cumulative incidence duration

questions.question: What is the reported VAP mortality per INICC data, and what accounts for variation across studies?
options: A: Identical regardless of the causative pathogen or population studied
B: 36.6% (up to 50%), varying by country, ICU type, criteria and organism
C: A uniform 5% across all studies and settings reported worldwide
D: No measurable impact on mortality in any study of ventilated patients
E: Over 95% in all studies, whatever the setting or population studied
questions.explanation: INICC data report a VAP mortality of 36.6%, up to 50% in some studies; the variation reflects differences in country, ICU type, diagnostic criteria, illness severity, underlying conditions and causative organism. The other options misstate the figure or deny variation.
questions.topic: VAP mortality INICC 36.6 percent variation

questions.question: A systematic review examined the diagnostic accuracy of classic VAP indicators (fever, purulent secretions, leukocytosis, chest radiography, cultures, CPIS). What did it find?
options: A: These classic indicators had poor specificity for diagnosing VAP
B: They showed excellent specificity, making VAP diagnosis straightforward
C: The CPIS score was 100% specific and sensitive
D: Chest radiography alone had perfect diagnostic accuracy
E: No differences were found, as all indicators were equally excellent
questions.explanation: The review found the classic indicators had poor specificity for VAP diagnosis, contributing to ongoing controversy around diagnostic criteria. The other options overstate the accuracy of these indicators.
questions.topic: VAP diagnosis poor specificity clinical indicators CPIS

questions.question: What is the single most effective way to prevent VAP, and what role do NIV and high-flow oxygen play?
options: A: Silver-coated endotracheal tubes used universally, regardless of other measures
B: Avoiding or shortening intubation; NIV and high-flow oxygen help by avoiding re-intubation
C: Routine early tracheostomy in all patients, as the only measure with proven benefit
D: Universal prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics, with no role for NIV or high-flow oxygen
E: Selective digestive decontamination alone, with no role for avoiding or shortening intubation
questions.explanation: The most effective prevention is avoiding intubation or minimising ventilation duration; NIV and high-flow oxygen help by avoiding intubation and re-intubation and shortening ventilation. The other options overstate unproven or controversial measures.
questions.topic: VAP prevention avoid intubation minimise duration NIV high flow oxygen

questions.question: Regarding measures to prevent VAP in intubated patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Head-of-bed elevation and an alcohol-based hand hygiene policy may help reduce VAP risk.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The role of stress ulcer prophylaxis, subglottic secretion drainage and selective digestive decontamination is well-established and free of controversy.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Oral care with chlorhexidine is one of the measures whose role in VAP prevention remains controversial.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Lightening sedation, using spontaneous breathing trials, timely extubation and early mobilisation all help reduce VAP risk.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is false - the role of measures including stress ulcer prophylaxis, subglottic secretion drainage and selective digestive decontamination remains controversial, not well-established.
questions.topic: VAP prevention bundle sedation mobilisation head of bed controversial measures

questions.question: Regarding risk factors for VAP caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A local MDR resistance rate above 25% is a recognised risk factor for MDR VAP.

statements.label: B
statements.text: ARDS before VAP and concurrent septic shock are recognised severity-related risk factors for MDR VAP.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A local MRSA rate below 5% is listed as the specific risk factor for MRSA-related VAP.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Parenteral antibiotics in the preceding 90 days and a hospital stay over 5 days before VAP are recognised risk factors.

questions.explanation: A, B and D are correct. C is reversed - the table lists a local MRSA rate above 20% (or unknown), not below 5%, as the specific MRSA risk factor.
questions.topic: VAP MDR risk factors local resistance antibiotic exposure MRSA threshold

questions.question: Which category of organisms causes most VAP, and which is the most common Gram-positive pathogen?
options: A: Viral pathogens are responsible for most VAP in intensive care
B: All VAP is caused by a single universal organism across all settings
C: Fungi cause most VAP in all populations of ventilated patients
D: Most cases are Gram-positive; Pseudomonas is the commonest Gram-negative organism
E: Mostly Gram-negative (Pseudomonas, Klebsiella); S. aureus the commonest Gram-positive
questions.explanation: Most VAP is caused by Gram-negative bacteria (Pseudomonas, E. coli, Klebsiella, Acinetobacter), with Staphylococcus aureus the most common Gram-positive pathogen. The other options misstate the microbiology.
questions.topic: VAP pathogens gram-negative Pseudomonas Staphylococcus aureus

questions.question: Fungi rarely cause VAP, with one exception. Which genus can cause late-onset VAP, and in which context particularly?
options: A: Histoplasma, particularly after cardiac surgery
B: Fungi have never been implicated in any case of VAP
C: Cryptococcus, particularly in immunocompetent young patients with no risk factors
D: Candida, particularly in diabetes mellitus
E: Aspergillus, particularly after recent influenza or in COVID-19-associated pneumonia
questions.explanation: Aspergillus is the notable fungal exception, implicated in late-onset VAP particularly after recent influenza or in COVID-19-associated pneumonia. The other options misidentify the organism or context.
questions.topic: fungal VAP Aspergillus late-onset influenza COVID-19

questions.question: Given high resistance rates and the competing needs to start appropriate treatment early yet avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum use, what approach is increasingly applied, and its benefit?
options: A: Rapid diagnostics, which have been shown to have no effect on antibiotic use or outcomes
B: Abandoning microbiological testing in favour of clinical judgement alone
C: Universal empirical treatment with one fixed regimen for all patients, with no testing
D: Withholding all antibiotics until final cultures return, whatever the severity
E: Rapid diagnostic testing for VAP, which can reduce antibiotic use and improve outcomes
questions.explanation: Rapid diagnostic testing for VAP is increasingly applied and can reduce antibiotic use and improve outcomes, easing the tension between early appropriate treatment and avoiding unnecessary broad-spectrum use. The other options misstate or deny its value.
questions.topic: VAP rapid diagnostics antibiotic resistance reduce use outcomes
